title: Making a spindle cop
subtitle: How to wind yarn onto a drop spindle to build a well-balanced, stable cop
excerpt: A cop is the conical package of spun yarn that builds up on the spindle shaft during spinning. A well-formed cop sits close to the whorl, balances the spinning weight, and releases cleanly for plying. This entry explains the winding pattern that produces a stable cop rather than a lumpy, unbalanced mass.
sourceNotes: Drop spindle cop building synthesised from general spindle spinning practice. The conical cop form is common to most traditional drop spindle traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: The conical or cylindrical package of spun singles wound directly onto the spindle shaft during spinning. A well-built cop sits close to the whorl and keeps the spindle balanced.

The disc at the top or bottom of the spindle shaft that provides momentum when the spindle is set spinning. The cop is built close to the whorl to keep the centre of gravity high and the spin efficient.

A beginner technique of stopping the spindle rotation (parking it under the arm or between the knees) and drafting the fibre before re-spinning. Useful when learning cop building, as both hands are free.
The single strand of yarn produced by one pass through spinning, before plying. The cop holds these singles ready for the next stage.

aliases: building a cop on drop spindle

content.text: onto the spindle shaft in a random heap. The result is a lopsided
that wobbles, slows, and forces frequent re-spinning. A properly wound cop is conical, sits tightly against the
, and allows the spindle to spin for longer per flick. Cop building can be practised separately from drafting using the
method, freeing both hands for the winding movement.

Tie a length of leader yarn (a short piece of pre-spun yarn) to the shaft just below the whorl with a half-hitch. Wind two or three times around the shaft close to the whorl, then bring the yarn up over the whorl and hook it into the notch or slip-loop at the top (for a top-whorl spindle). This starting position keeps the first wraps of the cop seated immediately under the whorl where they will be most stable.
Step 2: Build the base of the cop

After spinning a short length of singles, unhook from the notch and wind the yarn onto the shaft just below the whorl in a figure-of-eight or a tight, even coil. Three to four wraps at the base form a firm foundation. Keep the cop seated as close to the whorl as possible: yarn added far down the shaft lowers the centre of gravity and destabilises the spin.

As the cop grows, wind each new section in a diagonal direction, angling slightly away from the whorl with each pass. The resulting shape should be a shallow cone, widest at the base near the whorl and tapering toward the tip of the shaft. Return to the base with each new section of yarn rather than continuously spiralling downward: this builds a layered, interlocked structure that holds its shape under use.
Step 4: Finish and remove the cop

When the cop is full or spinning becomes noticeably sluggish, wind off onto a niddy-noddy or directly onto a storage skein. To remove the cop cleanly from the shaft, slide it off in the direction of the tip (away from the whorl). If it binds on the shaft, the cop may have been wound with too much tension: ease it off gently with a slight twisting motion. A well-wound cop slides off as a single compact unit.

items.problem: The spindle wobbles and slows quickly during spinning
items.cause: The cop is unevenly wound and heavier on one side
items.fix: Wind subsequent layers more evenly, rotating the spindle to distribute the yarn around the shaft. Keep layers close to the whorl to maintain a high centre of gravity.

items.problem: The cop slides down the shaft as it grows heavier
items.cause: The base wraps were too loose and did not grip the shaft
items.fix: Tie the leader yarn to the shaft with a firm half-hitch before beginning. The first few wraps should grip the shaft surface firmly.

items.problem: The cop collapses when removed from the spindle
items.cause: The cop was wound in parallel layers rather than a diagonal cone structure
items.fix: Rebuild using diagonal, crossing wraps. Each layer should cross over the previous one at an angle, creating a self-supporting interlocked structure.
